Beschreibung nennt die Hauptfunktionen (balances, txs, internal txs, ERC20, logs) und benennt die API-Version klar. Kontext klar, Domaene eindeutig.

Sehr detaillierte Tool-Beschreibungen mit Cross-References, korrekte Adresslaengen-Constraints, klare Pagination-Defaults, vollstaendige Output-Schemata mit Feldbeschreibungen. Exzellent dokumentiert.
Beschreibung knapp aber komplett: forward + reverse + autocomplete. Klare Anwendungsfaelle.

lat/lon mit Range-Constraints (-90..90, -180..180), Output-Format als GeoJSON FeatureCollection dokumentiert, coordinates-Reihenfolge [lon,lat] explizit erklaert, Cross-Reference autocomplete<->forwardGeocode.
Description is specific (DCAT-AP.de, SPARQL aggregate analytics) and lists concrete capabilities (themes, publishers, formats, keywords, recent). Quantification of 177k+ entries adds clarity.

Each tool has clear descriptions, well-bounded parameters with min/max/default constraints, and example tests. Theme codes example (ENVI, ECON, HEAL) helps LLM understand expected values; cross-references between tools aid orchestration.
Very specific: source (infraVelo), city (Berlin), domain (cycling infrastructure), data elements (bike lanes, parking, traffic signals), output format (GeoJSON), and license noted.

District param has clear slug format (lowercase, hyphenated) with three concrete Berlin districts. Output GeoJSON structure well documented. Cross-reference to discover district names via getAllProjects.
Excellent specificity: same shared description as part1 — Infura JSON-RPC across 12 named EVM chains with concrete capability list including event logs (relevant for this part).

Single tool with address regex, block-range constraint (max 10000 blocks noted), chain enum, and USDC test case. Drawback: no param-level descriptions; lacks topics filter parameter (only address-based).
Clear source (IPU Parline), domain (parliamentary data), explicit entities (countries, parliaments, chambers, elections, specialized bodies), and scale (190+ countries).

Every parameter has a description, ISO 2-letter codes enforced via min/max(2) with examples (DE, US, GB), election filter enum (lower/upper/unicameral), JSON:API pagination conventions documented, and rich cross-tool references.
Sehr spezifische Beschreibung: API-Version, Filteroptionen (taxonomy/country/habitat/threat), Skalierungs-Info (150k+ Arten). Klar abgegrenzter Use Case fuer Biodiversitaets-Queries.

Tool descriptions enthalten alle wichtigen Codes und Beispiele (CR/EN/VU/etc., Habitats 1-5, Threats 1/2/5/11). Enums werden semantisch erklaert. Parameter haben sinnvolle Defaults und optional-Markierungen.
Klare Beschreibung: NASA JPL Sentry Impact Risk Assessment mit konkreten Datenpunkten (probability, Palermo scale, virtual impactors). Domain-spezifisch und eindeutig.

Sehr gute Parameter-Descriptions mit Beispielen (2099942=Apophis, 1e-3 for 0.1%), klare Filterunits. Kleiner Schwachpunkt: Output-Felder im Summary haben keine description (h, ip, ps_cum etc. sind nur Codes).
Spezifische Beschreibung mit Provider (Bundesnetzagentur), Skalierung (175k+ Stationen), Datentyp (ArcGIS FeatureServer), und Filteroptionen (location, city, state, operator, power).

Tool descriptions erklaeren SQL WHERE-Syntax, deutsche Feldnamen werden benannt (Ort/Bundesland/Betreiber). queryByLocation enthaelt konkrete bounding-box-Berechnungsanleitung (~2km Box). Output-Felder sind gut dokumentiert. Parameter ohne individual descriptions kostet 0.5 Punkte.
Sehr konkret: Datentypen (books/photos/maps/manuscripts/audio/video), Skalierung (170M+ records). Klarer Mediendaten-Fokus.

Tool descriptions erklaeren fa-Parameter-Syntax mit Beispielen, Cross-References (listCollections -> searchCollection), Output-Schemas mit detaillierten Property-Descriptions inkl. Pagination. Parameter ohne individual descriptions verhindert die 5.0.
Specific: Meteora DLMM pools on Solana, with concrete data types (pools, pair groups, OHLCV, volume history, protocol metrics). Clear scope for liquidity/DEX analysis.

Strong cross-references between tools, pagination bounds defined, timeframe enums for OHLCV/volume, length constraints for Solana addresses, named real test addresses for multiple pools, and rich output schemas with field-level descriptions.
Description names the API (NASA Exoplanet Archive), the protocol (TAP/ADQL), dataset size (6100+), and lists searchable dimensions. Could mention typical use scenarios more explicitly.

Each route has clear descriptions, parameter placeholders, well-documented output schemas with units (parsecs, Kelvin, days), and good test examples. Slight weakness: ADQL parameter substitution semantics (SEARCH_TERM, METHOD enum values) are implicit.

Description is concrete — names the provider, the data types (station locations, availability, system info), the geographic coverage (50+ German networks with examples), and the access model (free, no API key). Very actionable.

Routes have cross-references (getAllNetworks → city uid → getStationsAndBikes), good output schemas including coordinate/capacity fields, and useful tests with specific city IDs. Both routes use the same path which may confuse some clients but is documented.
Description specifies provider (Neynar), protocol context (Farcaster, REST), and lists concrete operations (by username/FID/ETH address, casts, feeds). Clear enough to route to.

Cross-references between routes are excellent (use getUsersByFid → getUserCasts). Type enums (hash|url), bounded pagination, optional viewer_fid documented. Output schemas are descriptive but slightly thin (object descriptions vs structured properties).

Description names both capabilities (geocode + reverse geocode) and the data source (OpenStreetMap). Slightly short — does not name the service (Nominatim) explicitly or mention rate-limit / usage policy.

Routes cover all 3 standard Nominatim modes with both free-form and structured query support, cross-references between search/reverse/lookup tools, country code parameter, and well-documented output schemas with OSM id/type semantics.
Description names the system (Ocean Biodiversity Information System), data types (occurrences, taxonomy, checklists, dataset metadata), and scale (100M+ records worldwide). Clear and actionable.

Routes have well-described cross-references between checklist/taxon/occurrence/dataset/node tools, WoRMS AphiaID context, geometry filter, and realistic test cases. Output schemas could be more complete for taxon details.
Specific description with entity types (postal codes, localities, streets, municipalities, admin divisions) and three country scopes (DE/AT/CH). Clear no-auth open data.

Tools cross-reference each other (germanFullTextSearch references country-specific tools). Consistent pagination. Country-specific tools have appropriate context (Bundeslaender, Cantons, etc.). Clear test examples show real postal codes.
Clear scope (NFT marketplace data) with specific data types (collection stats, listings, offers, events, metadata) and version (v2 API). Includes tool selection guidance.

Tools cross-reference each other (getCollection vs getCollectionStats). Chain enum is comprehensive (ethereum, polygon, arbitrum, optimism, base, avalanche, zora). Event types enum is clear. Output schemas not included but parameter context is good.
Very specific: real-time aircraft tracking, four data types (state vectors, flight history, arrivals/departures, waypoints), and data source (ADS-B receiver network).

Tools have descriptive parameter constraints (time intervals: 2h, 30d, 7d). Output schemas include field-level descriptions and array order documentation. Bounding box parameters have lat/lon ranges. ICAO24 and ICAO codes used consistently.
Description is concrete with scale (19M+ profiles), enumerates content types (publications, affiliations, fundings, peer reviews), and notes auth/cost — gives LLM clear trigger conditions.

All tools have descriptive route-specific descriptions, explicit chaining hints (e.g. 'Use returned ORCID paths in getRecord'), pagination params have defaults/limits, and tests demonstrate Solr query syntax. Output schemas are documented.
Description names PANGAEA, scale (400K+ datasets), domain (earth/environmental/climate/ocean/geoscience), and access pattern (georeferenced datasets). Clear scope for LLM trigger.
Topic and env are constrained enums with human-readable values; pagination params have min/max/defaults; tests cover diverse scenarios (keyword, date range, topic filter, DOI lookup, JSON-LD).
Description names Pegelonline, scope (German federal waterways), data type (real-time water levels, station data, measurements) and access (no API key) — clear trigger conditions.
Tools have rich chaining hints (use UUIDs/names from getStations in getStation), ISO 8601 duration format documented (P1D, P7D), enums constrained, defaults set. Tests demonstrate real station names.
Description names Pump.fun on Solana, content scope (meme token launches, coins, live streams, replies, creator profiles), and gives explicit chaining hint (getCoins → getCoinByMint).

Tools have rich descriptions naming output fields (bonding curve state, Raydium pool, etc.), strong enum constraints (sort/order/includeNsfw), min/max/defaults on pagination, and clear address format constraints (32-44 chars for Solana). Chaining hints across tools.
Description names PVGIS by JRC, scope (solar PV energy output, radiation, time series, horizon profiles), and abbreviation expansion (EU Photovoltaic Geographical Information System). Clear trigger.

Many params with explicit enum constraints (PV tech, mounting, radiation database, format), latitude/longitude pairing, defaults. Tests demonstrate real cities (Berlin, Rome, Munich, etc.) and varied parameter usage.
Description is specific with concrete data points (250+ countries) and lists key data fields (names, capitals, currencies, languages, flags, geographic data). Clear scope, no ambiguity.

Each tool has descriptive paths, parameter types (enum for region), helpful defaults, three diverse tests per tool, and cross-references between tools. Field parameter convention is clearly explained.
Concrete counts (800+ characters, 50+ episodes, 120+ locations), explicit filter dimensions, and clear statement about free access without API key.
Strong parameter design with proper enums (status, gender), human-readable values, optional flags, pagination defaults, and meaningful test examples covering search and pagination scenarios.
Detailed scope (SEC EDGAR XBRL, all US public companies), specifies CIK format with concrete example (CIK0000320193), notes free access and User-Agent header requirement.

Excellent description of CIK format with concrete examples (Apple, Tesla). Lists common us-gaap concepts (Assets, Liabilities, NetIncomeLoss, etc.) and warns about case-sensitivity. Cross-references between tools.
Concrete scale (200M+ papers), specific features listed (TLDR summaries, citation intent, embeddings, author profiles), and access info documented.

Supports multiple ID formats (S2, DOI, ArXiv, PMID, PMCID, ACL, Corpus), helpful default field sets, cross-references between paper/author tools, and real examples (Attention Is All You Need, Geoffrey Hinton).
Identifies source with former name (Sensor.Community formerly Luftdaten.info), lists measured variables (PM2.5, PM10, temp, humidity) and scope (thousands of DIY sensors worldwide).

Strong enum for sensor types with common ones noted in description, location filters (area, bbox, country) clearly documented with units (km, ISO codes), and diverse tests covering different German cities and countries.
Description is specific (NORAD TLE orbital data, ISS, weather satellites) and conveys clear use cases. Concise and unambiguous.
Both tools have clear descriptions, sensible defaults, enum values for sort, cross-reference hint (use IDs from searchSatellites), and three concrete tests with real NORAD IDs.
Clear scope (momentum indicators), explicit indicator list (RSI, MACD, etc.), and notes local computation. Very informative for tool selection.

Each tool has crisp descriptions with interpretation notes (overbought/oversold ranges), sensible defaults, and concrete test data. Closings as CSV string is consistent but lacks per-parameter description fields.
Description specifies OHLC requirement and lists indicators (ADX, ATR, CCI, etc.). Helpful disambiguation from price-only indicators.
Each tool clearly enumerates required H/L/C arrays as CSV strings, lists thresholds and defaults, and provides matched sample candle data in tests. Minor: per-parameter descriptions missing.
Description clearly scopes trend-following indicators, names all variants, and emphasizes local computation. Disambiguates from momentum/volume schemas.
Tool descriptions explain each MA variant with use cases. Defaults are appropriate (14, 26, 12). Tests have aligned closings + intervals. Per-parameter description fields are not used though.
Clearly scopes volatility indicators with all four members enumerated and notes local computation. Concise and specific.
Each tool describes the indicator interpretation (band expansion, squeeze, outlier robustness) and provides defaults (20, 2) plus aligned test data. Slight gap: no per-parameter description text.
Description clearly scopes volume+candle indicators, lists members (OBV, VWAP, AC, PSAR, ZigZag), and notes local computation.
Tool descriptions explain data requirements (OHLCV vs HLCV), interpretation rules, and provide defaults plus aligned tests. Per-parameter descriptions are not used but key names are self-explanatory.
Clear scope: advanced momentum indicators with multi-library selection (indicatorts/trading-signals/talib). Lists 8 indicators. Useful for picking when basic momentum is insufficient.

Each tool clearly documents library enum with default and allowed values per indicator. Tests cover multiple library options, all parameters have sensible defaults, descriptions include interpretation thresholds.
Description states basic momentum scope, multi-library selection, lists 8 indicators (RSI, MACD, Stochastic etc.), and local computation note. Clear differentiation from advanced variant.
Library enum with default per tool, sensible defaults, multi-library tests, and threshold interpretation in descriptions. Consistent CSV closings pattern.
Description clearly scopes advanced trend indicators (DMA, KAMA, MAMA, T3, Linear Regression family) with library selection (trading-signals/talib). Local computation noted.
Each tool documents library options, sensible defaults, and clear parameter naming. Tests cover multiple libraries with matched closings arrays.
Description clearly scopes basic moving averages (SMA, EMA, DEMA, WMA, TEMA, TRIMA, WSMA, RMA) with multi-library selection. Clear differentiation from advanced trend.
Library enum with per-tool defaults and values lists, sensible period defaults, multi-library tests, consistent CSV closings format.
Description clearly scopes trend channels/bands (Bollinger, Keltner, Donchian, Acceleration Bands, Ichimoku) with multi-library selection. Specific membership listing.
Each tool has descriptions and library enum with defaults. Required candle arrays clearly enumerated. Sensible defaults for periods and deviation multipliers.
Clear scope: volatility indicators (ATR, TR, NATR, IQR, MAD, Ulcer Index, Mass Index, Chandelier Exit) with multi-library selection. Local computation noted.
Each tool has library enum and sensible defaults. Tools requiring HLC data clearly enumerate the arrays. Multi-library test coverage present.
Clear scope: volume-based indicators (OBV, VWAP, AD, ADOSC, CMF, MFI, VWMA, VPT) with multi-library selection. Local computation noted.
Each tool documents required OHLCV/HLCV arrays and library enum with sensible defaults. Multi-library tests present with matched test data.
Description specifies UniProt scope (250M+ entries, sequences/functions/classifications/cross-refs). Concise and clearly delimits the protein-data use case.

Tools document query syntax patterns (gene:TP53 AND reviewed:true), accession parameter, format enum, pagination via cursor, cross-references between search and entry. Real-world test queries provided.

Description is specific and clear: names data source (Library of Congress), enumerates entity types (bills, members, committees, etc.), specifies coverage (93rd Congress to present), and includes auth note (DEMO_KEY).
Parameters are well-typed with enums for billType/chamber, explicit ranges, defaults, and clear inline descriptions of congress numbering and bill type codes. Tests demonstrate canonical usage.
Specific provider (Wikidata), scope (100M+ items, structured knowledge), interface (SPARQL + predefined queries), auth (no key required). Comprehensive overview.

Mix of predefined queries (countriesByPopulation, citiesByCountry, etc.) and free-form SPARQL. Country IDs documented with examples (Q183=Germany, Q30=USA). querySparql tests show realistic SPARQL with label service usage.
Specific: provider (0x Protocol v2), domain (DEX aggregator/swaps), scope (100+ sources, 20+ chains), approval flows (Allowance Holder, Permit2). Comprehensive.

Parameters have min(1) validation, slippageBps range (0-10000) with default 100, clear separation of price vs quote and Allowance Holder vs Permit2 flows. Tests use realistic chain IDs and contract addresses across Ethereum, Polygon, Base, Arbitrum.
Specific: provider (zvg-portal.de), scope (foreclosure auctions all 16 German states), data types (auction dates, property details, valuations, court info).

Excellent inline documentation: enums for all 16 state codes with full names, property type codes (1-18) with descriptions, sort options enumerated. Cross-tool reference from search to detail. Tests cover multiple Bundeslaender.
Concrete scope: OGC SensorThings API, Hamburg, named sub-domains (E-charging, StadtRad, traffic, energy). Mentions OData features and no-API-key constraint.

OData params well documented with defaults/max, $expand and $filter examples like substringof('StadtRad',name) are concrete. Cross-tool flow (find IDs → expand Datastreams → getObservations) explained.
Clear use-case (detect honeypot tokens), specific signals checked (buy/sell tax, liquidity, contract risks), and target (EVM token address). Concrete API source named.

Single parameter with regex validation for EVM address format, supported chains listed (Ethereum, BSC, Base), three concrete test cases (USDC, USDT, WETH addresses). Rich output schema aids interpretation.
Specific official source (insolvenzbekanntmachungen.de), domain (German insolvency), explicit search dimensions listed, and structured output noted.

Full Bundesland code mapping (0-15) and subject-type enum (0-7) in tool description, registerzeichen enum, German param names matched to portal. Date format not made explicit; tests provide concrete usage examples.

Very specific description: scope (FEC campaign finance), entity types (candidates, committees, filings, totals), time range (1980-present), elections (presidential, Senate, House), and access info (API key, DEMO_KEY).

Tools have clear distinct purposes. Enum P/S/H is documented in getCandidateTotals description. Inconsistent: getElectionSummary uses readable enum (president/senate/house) while others use codes (P/S/H). Sort syntax (-receipts) explained via default value.
Description specifies scale (20M+ editions, works, authors, subjects) and source (Internet Archive). Clear scope but could mention what data is queryable.

Excellent cross-references between tools (use work keys from searchBooks in getWorkByKey). Output schemas have field-level descriptions with examples (e.g. /works/OL18417W). Enums for sort and ebooks are clear. Multiple test examples.
Klar und spezifisch: nennt drei Datenarten (coins, NFTs, categories) und enthaltene Metriken (price, volume).
Drei Tools alle ohne Parameter, aber Output-Schema sehr ausfuehrlich dokumentiert mit Feldbeschreibungen, Sentiment-Erklaerungen und Beispielwerten (BTC, ETH).
Clear functional scope (holidays, Shabbat times, conversion), enumerates which event classes are covered (major/minor/fast days/Torah readings), and notes free access.

Params well documented with enum on/off, default values, and concrete GeoName IDs (Jerusalem, NYC, London) plus convert examples. Slight reliance on opaque flag conventions (maj, min, nx, ss) but inline description explains them.
Klar und spezifisch: nennt Stablecoins, market metrics, peg stability und historische Trends - sehr fokussierter Use-Case.
Parameter sehr gut beschrieben mit Beispielen, Defaults und Constraints (1-250, 1-90 days); enum mit konkreten Stablecoin-IDs; Output-Schema umfangreich aber Felder ohne descriptions.
Spezifisch: nennt zwei Operationen (search + OHLC) und Use-Case (technical analysis), API-Tier-Hinweis enthalten.
Sehr gut: Parameter mit Beschreibungen, Granularitaets-Logik (1-2 days = 30min etc.) erklaert, Tool-Verkettung (searchCoins -> getCoinOhlc) explizit, Output-Schema vollstaendig dokumentiert.
Beschreibung klar: englische Woerterbuch-API mit Definitions, Phonetik, Synonyme, Antonyme, Beispiele. Spezifisch und fokussiert.
Nur ein Parameter (word), aber Output-Schema hervorragend dokumentiert mit phonetics-Array, meanings-Struktur (partOfSpeech, definitions, examples), erklaert Array-Charakter durch Etymologie.
Beschreibung klar: GDACS fuer Naturkatastrophen, Liste der Typen (earthquake, flood, cyclone, volcano, wildfire, drought), inkl. Alert-Levels und Geo-Daten.

Enum-Werte (EQ, FL, TC, VO, WF, DR) und alertlevel (green/orange/red) sind klar. Output dokumentiert eventtype-Codes. Datumsformat fuer fromDate/toDate ist nicht explizit dokumentiert, aber Tests zeigen ISO-Datum.
Beschreibung klar: Lil Nouns DAO Governance-Proposals via Goldsky Subgraph. Beide Operationen (list/byId) explizit.

Status-Enum-Werte komplett dokumentiert (PENDING/ACTIVE/...), Output-Felder klar erlaeutert (forVotes, quorumVotes, startBlock), GraphQL-Query als optional/required vorhanden. id-Regex bestaetigt numerisches Format.
Clear scope (Project Gutenberg, 70k+ ebooks) and explicit filter dimensions named (author, language, subject, downloads). Slightly terse but unambiguous.

Tools have clear descriptions, enum/default values for sort, well-chosen test examples (e.g., classic book IDs 1342, 76, 2701), and cross-references guide multi-step flows. Comma-separated batch IDs documented.
Specific scope (HathiTrust, 17M+ volumes), explicit identifier types (ISBN, OCLC, LCCN) and source (research libraries). Clear and actionable.
Single-parameter lookups with clear semantic mapping per ID type, normalization notes (ISBN-10/13, LCCN), and concrete test IDs. Output schema explains rights codes and htid structure.
Specific German energy registry (MaStR) with concrete dataset size (8.4M+), data types (electricity/gas generation/consumption), and core features (filtering, pagination). Clear scope.

Filter syntax explicitly documented with example (FieldName~operator~value), cross-references to discovery endpoint (getFilterColumns*), pagination bounds set, and output schema includes German field names with English descriptions. Filter format may still be tricky for LLM without examples.
Specific: NASA APOD with concrete output (titles, explanations, high-res URLs) and start date (1995). Adequate scope.

Three clear access modes (single, range, random), pagination bounds, output schemas with field descriptions including media_type and copyright. API key parameter handled via placeholder. Realistic test dates. Could note YYYY-MM-DD format constraint on date param.

Description names the federal system (NINA), data types (DWD weather, MOWAS civil protection, BIWAPP, KATWARN), and the country context (German federal warning system). Slightly less detailed than ninaWarnApp counterpart.

Routes have helpful cross-references documenting when to use each one, severity/urgency enums noted in output descriptions. Weakness: KATWARN output schema is just an array of strings — unclear if intentional or thin.
Clear scope of citation data with specific query types (DOI-to-DOI links, references, counts, metadata) and licensing info (CC0). Well-targeted description.

Excellent cross-references between tools (getCitations vs getReferences, getCitationCount). Output schemas include field descriptions. OCI concept explained in description. Single DOI parameter is simple and clear.
Description lists six distinct services (routing, isochrones, matrix, geocoding, elevation, optimization) and data source (OpenStreetMap). Clear and broad scope.

Tools cover varied use cases with comprehensive profile enum. Coordinate format (longitude first) only shown via tests. Optimization parameters (jobs/vehicles) lack inline schema docs. Generally good cross-tool consistency.
Beschreibung nennt klar trading pairs, Datenarten und Filterungsmoglichkeiten.
Sehr klare Filter-Parameter mit Beispielen (BTC vs bitcoin; USDT vs tether); Unterscheidung zwischen Symbol und ID gut dokumentiert.
Very clear description with concrete content numbers (320+ spells, 300+ monsters, 12 classes, 9 races), the SRD scope, edition (5e), and no-API-key requirement.

Tool descriptions are strong, cross-reference each other (use listClasses to find index values), and output schemas are richly documented. However, top-level parameter inputs (index, name) lack per-parameter descriptions, only sample values via tests.
Clear description listing the response types (instant answers, abstracts, related topics, definitions) and noting no API key. Could mention limitations like no general web search.
Tool description guides LLM to check Type field. Output schema documents all response Type codes (A/D/C/N/E) explicitly. Boolean-like enums (0/1) with defaults are clear.
Beschreibung ist praezise: nennt vier konkrete Operationen (verify personal/EIP-712, hash, recover) und stellt klar, dass kein RPC/API-Key noetig ist. Kontext fuer Anwendungsfall ist eindeutig.
Jede Route hat klare Beschreibungen, Cross-References (z.B. hashMessage->recoverAddress) und Regex-Validierung fuer hex. Parameter-Beschreibungen pro Feld fehlen jedoch, nur z-Constraints gegeben.
Very specific: source (LHP), data type (real-time flood), quantification (~1,200 stations), geographic scope (16 federal states), and two output types (current readings + warnings).

States param uses standard German state abbreviations with concrete examples (BY, NW, SN, TH). Flood class enum well documented (0-4 mapping). Minor: only one parameter per tool limits ambiguity but param description itself is sparse.
Description is specific with concrete dataset scale (250K+ decisions, 57K+ law sections, 1100+ law books), data type (German legal data), licensing (CC0). Clear scope.

Clear distinct purposes per tool (search vs list). Tools collectively cover cases, laws, books, and states. Pagination is consistent. Simple text query parameter with no syntax docs but basic free-text appears adequate.
Clear geographic scope (all 50 states, DC, Puerto Rico) and entity types (legislators, bills, committees, votes). Concise and well-targeted.

listJurisdictions tool helps discover valid jurisdiction values. Geo lookup using lat/lng is well-modeled. Free-text subject/party parameters have no enums, but discovery is supported. Pagination consistent.
Description names provider and core capabilities (search by location/magnitude/date/depth, GeoJSON). Clear and specific but compact.

Parameters are exemplary: precise min/max ranges for lat/lon/magnitude, enums for orderby and alertlevel, well-documented output schema with detailed property descriptions and Did You Feel It, ShakeMap context. Multiple realistic tests.
Specific: Morpho Blue lending markets via GraphQL with concrete capabilities (markets, positions, chains) and explicit chain support list (Ethereum, Base, Polygon, Arbitrum, Optimism).

Chain IDs are explicit enum values, pagination bounds defined, regex validation for wallet addresses. Tool names are semantic. However, no output schemas defined, and the underlying GraphQL query bodies are hidden — LLM only sees high-level params. Tests are realistic with actual market keys.
Clear: names EMSC and FDSN, lists filter dimensions (magnitude, location, depth, time). Could mention data freshness or coverage area.

Tool descriptions reference output format (GeoJSON FeatureCollection), example event IDs (20190330_0000065), and cross-references between queryEvents and getEventById. Constraints (max 12000) and defaults documented.

Die Beschreibung ist konkret und nennt klar Zielgruppe (deutsche Politiker), Datentypen (Profile, Parlamentsdaten, Mandate, Kandidaturen, Abstimmungen) sowie Quelle und Lizenz (Abgeordnetenwatch, CC0). Es fehlt allerdings ein expliziter Use-Case-Trigger (wann statt eines anderen Tools zu nutzen) und eine Abgrenzung, was die API nicht abdeckt.

Alle Parameter haben aussagekraeftige Beschreibungen mit Typ, optional/required, sinnvollen Defaults und Limits (z.B. max 1000, 0-based pagination). Hilfreiche Beispiele in Tests (Politician IDs, parliament IDs) und Hinweise wie 'show_information' fuer related_data erleichtern korrekte LLM-Aufrufe. Kleine Schwaeche: Parameter wie 'related_data' geben nur ein Beispiel und keine vollstaendige Enum-Liste, parliament-IDs werden nicht durchgaengig erklaert.

Die Beschreibung ist klar und spezifisch: nennt den Datentyp (Real-time air quality), Skalierung (10,000+ stations), konkrete Metriken (AQI, PM2.5, PM10, O3, NO2, SO2, CO) und Zusatz (health recommendations). Kein dediziertes skill whenToUse vorhanden, was Use-Case-Triggering leicht einschraenkt; die Beschreibung selbst bleibt aber praezise.

Alle Parameter haben klare Beschreibungen mit konkreten Beispielen (beijing, 52.52/13.405 fuer Berlin, station ID 1437). Typen sind sauber (string/number), Token-Parameter ist als server-injected gekennzeichnet, und jede Route hat aussagekraeftige Tests sowie ein detailliertes Output-Schema. Minimaler Abzug, da Wertbereiche/Format-Constraints (z.B. Koordinatengrenzen, Keyword-Mindestlaenge) nicht explizit dokumentiert sind.
Very specific description with concrete data source (BfS), exact scope (gamma dose rate, ~1,700 stations), frequency (10 min), and geographic coverage (Germany). Excellent triggerability.

User-relevant parameters (maxFeatures, startIndex, viewparams) have clear constraints (min/max/default). Tests provide concrete station IDs as examples. WFS-internal params lack descriptions but are fixed values, not user-facing.
Names data source (Catalogue of Life ChecklistBank), scope (taxonomic names), volume (2.07M+ species), and coverage (all kingdoms). Very specific.

Parameters lack description fields but enums are human-readable (prefix/whole_words/exact, accepted/synonym/misapplied, rank/environment/nametype). Concrete biological examples in tests (Panthera leo, Homo sapiens). Pagination clear.
Names provider (Chainlist), scope (EVM chain metadata), and concrete data types returned (chain IDs, RPC, explorers, WebSocket, native currency). Highly triggerable for blockchain config lookup.

Parameters lack description fields but constraints (min(1), min(2)) and test examples (chain_id: 1 for Ethereum, 137 for Polygon, 'eth'/'zk' keywords) are clear. Output schema well-annotated. test_speed as string seems odd but defaulted.
Description is clear: names tech (Vega-Lite), chart types (candlestick/line/multi-line/bar), output format (base64 PNG), and key feature (local rendering, no external API).

Parameters lack description fields but tool descriptions explain input format (comma-separated, JSON array for multi-line series). Tests demonstrate exact CSV format with concrete values. Width/height constraints clear. Title defaults sensible.
Klar und prazise, beschreibt Datenarten (prices, market caps, supply, volume, historical) und Abdeckung (thousands of tokens).
Parameter haben gute Beschreibungen mit Beispielen (bitcoin, ethereum), enum-Werte fur interval mit Erklarung; Output-Schema-Felder selbst sind aber nicht beschrieben (nur Typen).

Klar formuliert, nennt drei Operationen (list, info, market pairs - allerdings nur 2 echte exchange-Tools + 2 weitere); enthaelt etwas Inkonsistenz (4 Tools im Schema, Description erwaehnt nur exchanges).
Parameter durchgehend mit klaren Beschreibungen, enum-Werten, Beispielen (270 fuer Binance, BTC,ETH); Defaults gut definiert; Output-Schema-Felder ohne descriptions aber meist verstaendlich.
Concise, specific description mentioning provider, free-tier limit, and all four endpoint purposes.
Few parameters but well-described with examples (e.g. 'Cryptorank currency ID (e.g. 1 for Bitcoin)'), constraint min(1), and clear cross-references in tool descriptions.
Clear and focused description, lists exactly what data is provided (CRV supply, crvUSD supply, AMM volumes).
All three endpoints are parameterless and well-described; simplicity matches the API and leaves little ambiguity.
Specific description mentioning 21 EVM chains and the data types (TVL, coins, gauge info, etc.); concise and informative.
Blockchain and registry enums fully enumerated with descriptions and examples; tool descriptions mention registry options like 'main, factory, crypto'.
Concise, specific description naming source (dawum.de) and content (German election polls, institutes, parliaments).
All endpoints are parameterless, well-described, with output fields documented (Forsa, INSA examples); cross-references between endpoints stated.
Concise description listing capabilities (bridge quotes, chains, tokens, orders) and naming the protocol (DeBridge DLN).
Every parameter has descriptions with chain ID examples (1=Ethereum, 137=Polygon), amount unit hint (wei), and orderId regex validator.
Specific domain (boosted tokens on DexScreener), names two distinct endpoints (latest, most active) and scope (across all DEX chains).
Both endpoints are parameterless; output schema documents each field with semantics (boost amount, header image, etc.); tool descriptions however generic.
Specific scope listing four endpoints (profiles, search, token-to-pair, pools) and source (DexScreener).
Every parameter has descriptions with concrete examples (ethereum, bsc, solana, query examples); outputs fully documented.
Clear and focused description naming Dune Analytics, the function (fetch latest cached result by query ID), and the return type. Compact, single-purpose schema.

Single parameter query_id is unambiguous. Tool description names the prerequisite (query must have been executed). Output schema documents state codes (QUERY_STATE_COMPLETED etc.) and metadata richly.
Clear and specific: lists all conversion utilities (wei/ether, hexlify, UTF-8), notes ethers.js source and offline use.

Tool descriptions cite token-specific decimals (6 for USDC/USDT, 8 for WBTC) which guides correct calls. Numeric bounds (0..77) on decimals are explicit. Per-parameter descriptions missing and output schemas generic, but tool-level guidance is strong.
Clear and specific: lists all utilities (keccak256, Solidity packed, CREATE2/CREATE, ENS namehash, EIP-55 checksum), notes ethers.js source and offline use.

Tool descriptions are clear with concrete use cases (Uniswap pool prediction). Strict regex validation for address (^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$) and bytes32 (^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{64}$). Per-parameter descriptions missing, output schemas generic, but tool guidance and validation are strong.
Beschreibung praezise: Gas-Oracle (safe/proposed/fast) ueber multiple EVM-Chains. Explizit unterstuetzte Chains genannt. Klar fokussiert.

Klare Enum-Werte fuer chainName (human-readable), Cross-Reference zwischen getGasOracle und estimateGasCost. Gasprice als string ohne Hinweis auf Wei-Format koennte klarer sein, aber Tests zeigen Wei-Werte.
Sehr spezifisch: Provider (hbz), Skalierung (250M+ holdings, GND), Datenfelder, Format (REST/JSON-LD). Klar abgegrenzter Use Case fuer deutsche Bibliotheksdaten.

Tool descriptions sind klar mit Lucene/Filter-Syntax-Beispielen. Tests zeigen Field-spezifische Queries. Schwaeche: keine individuellen Parameter-descriptions, Output-Schemas sind dünn (member:array of object ohne nested properties).

Description is specific and clear — names the source satellite (DSCOVR), the camera (EPIC), data types (natural, enhanced, aerosol, cloud), and what is returned (metadata, coordinates). Slightly weakened by absence of explicit use-case framing.

Each tool has a clear description, paths and required params; date routes show example dates in tests. Weakness: no explicit date format (YYYY-MM-DD) noted in descriptions/parameters, only inferable from test values.
Description clearly names the API (NeoWs) and three usage modes (close approach feed, lookup, browse). Brief but specific. Could mention the 7-day feed limit at description level.

Tools have clear descriptions, sensible defaults (page/size), min/max bounds, optional flags, and varied test examples. Date format is shown via tests. The 7-day max is mentioned in getFeed description.

Description names the agency (NWS, NOAA), the API (Weather.gov), the country scope (US), and three concrete data dimensions (alerts by state, forecast by grid, station observations). Clear and actionable.

Tools have clear descriptions and realistic test examples for major US cities. The chained workflow (points → grid → forecast) is implicit but inferable. Weakness: no output schemas at all, only minimal descriptions in tool definitions.
Description is specific with scale (170M+ products), entity types (publications, datasets, software, projects), and key filters (EU funding, open access, impact metrics). Clear when to use.

Tool descriptions disambiguate overlapping tools (searchPublications vs searchResearchProducts) and parameter names are self-documenting. Enums for funder/influence/popularity are explicit. Date format for fromDateAccepted not specified.
Description is specific with scale (240M+) and clearly lists entity types (works, authors, institutions, funders, topics). Communicates open and free nature.

Tools cross-reference each other (use IDs from searchWorks in getWork). Filter syntax shown in tests (publication_year:2024,is_oa:true). However, the generic filter/sort string parameters lack documented options/syntax in parameter descriptions.
Clear use cases (screen persons/companies, retrieve entities, dataset metadata) and data types (sanctions, PEP, criminal records). API name (yente) specified.

Tools have distinct purposes well-described. Algorithm enum is comprehensive. queries body parameter for matchEntities uses raw JSON string which is awkward. Schema/topics/datasets parameters lack value documentation but tools cover catalog discovery.
Specific description with network scale (16K+ stations), measurements (air quality, temperature, humidity, UV, noise), and provenance (re:edu, University of Muenster). Worldwide scope.

Tools are clear with exposure enum (indoor/outdoor/mobile/unknown). bbox format documented in searchBoxesByLocation description. UPPERCASE template placeholders are inconsistent with other schemas. Test examples illustrate usage well.
Description is clear and specific — names Orca DEX on Solana, lists exactly what data is fetched (pool data, token listings, whirlpool details), and gives explicit usage guidance with tool chaining.

Tools have clear descriptions naming returned fields and a chaining hint. Address parameter has min/max length constraints but lacks an explicit description; getWhirlpools/getTokens have no parameters which simplifies usage.
Description names domain (German federal waterway gauging stations), data type (water levels, measurements, station data) and source (WSV). Slightly less explicit than the sister schema.
Strong chaining hints (use UUIDs from getStations), timeseries default W with shortname explanation (W/Q), filters and pagination available. Tests cover real station UUIDs.
Description identifies source (German BVL), scope (pesticide registration database), and content categories (products, ingredients, companies, restrictions) — clear and specific trigger.

Tools have descriptive cross-referencing notes (cross-reference with getActiveIngredients) but parameters are all single optional ID-fields without explicit descriptions on parameter level. Field names like kennr/wirknr are German codes without inline explanation.
Description identifies POAP protocol, GraphQL access, and three usage modes (introspection, predefined queries, custom). Could elaborate slightly on POAP context (Proof of Attendance Protocol).

Predefined queryId is constrained by enum with 11 human-readable values; each test has descriptive purpose. Custom query parameter is a raw GraphQL string with example test. Strong tool chaining via predefined query catalog.
Description gives scale (4M+ podcasts) and concrete capabilities (search, trending, recent, live, categories). Clear, specific trigger conditions.

Tools have clear route-specific descriptions, params have min/max/defaults. However, output schemas are generic (just data: object), making field structure unclear. Tests demonstrate real feed IDs and languages.
Description names Polymarket CLOB API, scope (prediction markets), and two operations (list with filters, retrieve by condition ID). Clear and specific.
Compact schema with status enum (active/resolved), min/max/default for pagination, chaining hint ('Use condition_id from results in getMarketInfo'), and detailed output schemas.

Description names source (Regionaldatenbank Deutschland via GENESIS REST API), scale (160+ indicators), regional granularity (Kreis/Gemeinde/Bundesland), and auth note (free registration). Clear trigger.

Five well-structured tools with descriptive route purposes, search/filter parameters with constrained enums (de/en, code/content, search areas), pagination defaults, and tests showing real codes (12411-01-01-4). Auth credentials are properly externalized as server params.
Description names source (German Federal Foreign Office / Auswaertiges Amt) and content (travel advisories, country security info, diplomatic contacts). Clear and specific.

Four tools with chaining hints (use IDs from getAllWarnings in getWarningById). Parameter contentId is a single string. Tests use real ID. Parameter lacks explicit description but tool description compensates.
Concrete scope (63K+ articles), specifies access path (Crossref API), lists all filter dimensions (date, DOI, publisher, keyword), and clarifies data source.

Parameters have proper types, defaults, and enum constraints. However, fromDate/untilDate/memberId are marked location 'body' on GET requests, which is unusual and may confuse callers. Tests cover representative use cases.
Clearly identifies source (RKI) and lists data granularities (national, state, district, vaccination, testing). No counts but explicit dimensions.
Strong enum for state abbreviations (all 16 valid values), good cross-references between tools, descriptive output properties, and well-documented German-specific identifiers (AGS, Bundeslaender).
Concrete scale (100,000+ organizations), clear capability statement (search/retrieve/match), and identifies the data source (ROR). Could elaborate on use cases.
Excellent cross-references, advanced syntax explained (Elasticsearch query string with field syntax example types:education), real-world examples (CERN, MIT, Oxford), and clear pagination defaults.
Specific time range (1993 to present), explicit form examples (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, S-1), search dimensions noted, and access requirements documented.

Clear form descriptions in tool desc (10-K=annual, S-1=IPO). Date range requires both 'custom' enum and startdt/enddt — well-documented through test examples. Only one tool, limiting cross-reference patterns.
Clear capability list (open ports, CPEs, hostnames, tags, CVEs) and clear access info (no API key). Concrete IP-based use case.

Single tool with clear IP parameter, multiple well-known test IPs (Google DNS, Cloudflare, OpenDNS), and described output fields with semantics (cpes, tags, vulns). Could add IP format validation regex.
Clear data scope (native + ERC20 tokens with USD valuations) and realtime nature across EVM chains. Concise.
Address regex validation, full enum for chains (35+ chains), reasonable limit bounds (1-1000), and diverse tests across major chains (Ethereum, Base, Polygon).
Token types listed (native, SPL, SPL-2022) with USD valuation, scope (SVM chains) clear. Concise.
Base58 regex for Solana addresses, enum for chainName (SOLANA, ECLIPSE), reasonable limits, and meaningful tests on both chains.
Specifies NFT standards (ERC721, ERC1155), data returned (metadata, images, collection details), and scope (EVM chains).
EVM address regex, full enum (60+ chains including testnets, TRON), reasonable limit (1-2500), and tests covering Ethereum, Base, Polygon with real Vitalik address.
Clear list of metadata fields (symbol, name, decimals, USD price, logo URLs), and scope (native + ERC20 across EVM chains).
Smart regex allowing both EVM address and 'native' literal. Comprehensive chain enum (70+ chains). Tests demonstrate native ETH lookup and USDC on multiple chains.
Klare Datenquelle (Smithsonian Open Access, 11M+ Objekte) mit konkretem Scope (Museumsobjekte, Kunst, Artefakte). Use Cases bleiben implizit aber gut ableitbar.

Vollstaendig spezifizierte Endpoints mit Enum-Werten fuer sort/type/category, sinnvolle min/max-Bereiche, klare Beispiel-IDs (nasm_..., nmnh_...), aussagekraeftige Tests, gut beschriebene Outputs mit Unit-Codes.
Sehr praezise Beschreibung listet alle Preis-Modi (real-time, historisch, timestamp, ranges, batch multi-token) auf. Provider klar genannt.
Mehrere Endpoints mit klaren Naming-Konventionen. Token-Adressen und Timestamp-Parameter wahrscheinlich gut spezifiziert mit Validierung.
Klar (TED, EU-Procurement Notices, Expert-Query-Syntax). Wichtiger Use Case (EU-Vergaben) klar erkennbar.
Expert-Query-Syntax explizit dokumentiert mit Beispielen (CY = DEU, ND = 123456-2026), Country-Codes aufgezaehlt, sinnvolle Defaults (limit=10, page=1). Sehr nutzerfreundlich fuer LLM.
Description lists complementary VBB functions (nearby stops, trip search, line info, shapes, autocomplete) and notes it complements the core schema. Clear scope.
Tools have descriptions and cross-references; however some tests lack actual ID values (e.g. getTrip and getLine have only placeholder descriptions). Parameter naming clear and defaults reasonable.
Description clearly conveys 1200+ heritage sites with filterable dimensions (country, category, danger, inscription year). Free, public. Specific and concise.

where filter uses OpenDataSoft ODSQL syntax (e.g. states_names="Germany") with examples in tests. ISO code interpolation pattern works but is implicit. Pagination defaults sensible. Per-parameter descriptions missing.
Description is specific: region (Rhein-Ruhr), broader scope (Germany-wide via DELFI), capabilities (stops, departures, trip planning, alerts), auth (no key required).
Parameter descriptions provide defaults and EFA-specific names (name_sf, name_dm, type_dm). Tests demonstrate realistic usage. Some EFA parameters (anyObjFilter_sf=2) lack semantic explanation.
Specific: provider (Wikimedia Commons), scope (100M+ files), capabilities (search, browse, metadata, license/author), licensing (CC). Clear and complete.

MediaWiki API parameters are well documented with prefixes (File:, Category:) and namespace usage. Enums for cmtype. Some descriptions have minor typos (split sentence about File: prefix). Tests demonstrate canonical usage.
Specific: provider (WIPO IPCPUB), purpose (IPC symbol validity, classification details), and output fields described. Clear and focused.

Single tool with well-documented symbol format and version parameter. Tool description explains all output fields (K/I/D categories, V/N validity, version codes). Multiple test cases including invalid symbol and specific version.
Specific: provider (World Bank), scope (17,000+ indicators, 260+ countries), example domains (GDP, population, poverty, health).

Well-documented parameters: enum for incomeLevel, ranges for mrv/per_page, cross-tool ID discovery hints. Tests use realistic ISO codes and indicator IDs (NY.GDP.MKTP.CD, SP.POP.TOTL). Output schema captures two-element pagination/data array pattern.
Specific: provider (Yahoo Finance), data (OHLCV), generality (any ticker), no API key. Clear and concise.

Range and interval are well-defined enums with sensible defaults. Symbol is free string with realistic test examples (BTC-USD, ETH-USD, AAPL) covering crypto and equities. Output schema documents OHLCV array structure.
Beschreibung klar fokussiert: deutsche Feiertage, alle Bundeslaender, optionales Filtering. Knapp aber praezise.

getStateHolidays beschreibt valid State-Codes inline (BW, BY,...), Jahr-Default ist vorhanden, nur_land mit min/max(2). Output enthaelt detaillierte Doku ueber state-keys, ISO-date und hinweis-Felder.
Beschreibung praezise: 11M+ Namen, Postleitzahlen, Elevation, Timezones. Konkrete Funktionen (search, reverse, country info) klar.

Enum-Werte fuer featureClass/style/orderby klar, lat/lng als number(), Cross-References vorhanden. Aber featureClass-Codes (A,H,L,P...) ohne Erlaeuterung, Output-Doku teils generisch ('Fcl value', 'AdminCode1 value').

Clear and informative: explicit library name, 80+ indicators, named categories (momentum, trend, volume) with examples, and local computation. Slightly generic per-part description (same for all parts).

Each tool has indicator-domain description with formulas, sensible default periods (e.g., 14, 12, 26, 9). Drawback: parameter-level descriptions are missing and 'asset' strategy params are stringified JSON without explicit schema doc; tests do show structure.
Clear and informative: explicit library, 80+ indicators, named categories with examples, local computation. Generic per-part description shared with sibling parts.

Each indicator tool has formula/domain description with sensible defaults. Drawback: parameter-level descriptions missing; 'asset' strategy params are stringified JSON requiring inference from test samples.
Clear and informative: explicit library, 80+ indicators, named categories with examples, local computation. Generic per-part description shared with sibling parts.

Each indicator tool has formula/domain description with sensible defaults. Drawback: parameter-level descriptions missing; 'asset' strategy params are stringified JSON requiring inference from test samples.
Clear and informative: explicit library, 80+ indicators, named categories with examples, local computation. Generic per-part description shared with sibling parts.

Each indicator tool has formula/domain description with sensible defaults. Drawback: parameter-level descriptions missing; 'asset' strategy params are stringified JSON requiring inference from test samples.
Clear and informative: explicit library, 80+ indicators, named categories with examples, local computation. Generic per-part description shared with sibling parts.

Each indicator tool has formula/domain description with sensible defaults. Drawback: parameter-level descriptions missing; 'asset' strategy params are stringified JSON requiring inference from test samples.
Clear description naming the ECB SDMX REST API and listing covered data types (exchange rates, interest rates, monetary aggregates). Mentions time series filtering.

Tool descriptions explain the SDMX key pattern with concrete examples (D.USD.EUR.SP00.A) per dataflow. Cross-references between tools, enums for detail. Parameters lack individual descriptions but tool-level documentation is strong enough to guide LLM calls.
Clear and specific: lists all six utilities (decode/encode calldata, decode events, compute selectors, ABI en/decode), notes offline (no RPC/API key).

Tool descriptions are clear with examples (ERC-20 transfer, JSON array format hints). Parameters lack individual descriptions; LLM must infer from tool description + test. Output schemas generic ({result: object}). Regex validation for hex data enforced.
Clear scope of FDA datasets covered (drugs, devices, food, recalls, adverse events). Specifies clear use cases. Could mention search syntax.

Tools cross-reference each other (searchDrugEvents vs searchDrugLabels). Generic 'search' parameter requires FDA-specific syntax which is only shown by tests. Output schemas have field descriptions. Tests demonstrate field paths and operators (AND, range).
Klar formuliert, nennt zwei Operationen (list + get by ID) und Datentypen (volume rankings, metadata).
Parameter haben Beschreibungen mit Beispielen (binanceus, kraken); Output-Schema-Felder ohne Beschreibungen, aber Feldnamen sind selbsterklarend.
Klar formuliert, beschreibt drei Operationen (list, get by ID, products per exchange) und das Datenthema (derivatives).
Parameter haben Beschreibungen mit konkreten Beispielen (binance_futures, bybit); Output-Schema-Felder ohne descriptions aber Feldnamen meist verstaendlich.
Reasonable scope (ENS plus some EVM chains via ethers.js, forward and reverse). 'some EVM name services' is vague — listing supported chains in the description would help.
Explicit enum of 13 chain names, address regex/length validation, per-parameter descriptions with examples. No output schemas though, which slightly weakens LLM understanding of results.
Beschreibung praezise: gesetze-im-internet.de Index, 6,800+ Gesetze, mit Abkuerzung, Titel, Link. Klare Abgrenzung zum content-Schema.
Parameter klar (q, limit/offset Range, letter 1-Char). Tests mit echten Beispielen (GG, BGB, HGB). Output-Schema jedoch generisch (nur 'result: object'), keine Felder beschrieben.
Concise and clear scope (Hacker News, JSON feeds, list of feed kinds) but somewhat terse. Doesn't mention filters/quotas.

Enum exhaustively lists all feed paths (24 values), filter params have min/max constraints, and 5 varied test cases (front page, ask, search, favorites, replies). Slightly underdocumented description field semantics.
Clear scope: read-only contract calls via Infura on EVM mainnets, ABI-based signature. Slightly terse on which exact chains supported (deferred to enum).

Address regex, dynamic chain enum via shared list, function signature minimum length, and concrete USDC test case. Drawback: 'args' JSON-array-string format only implicit; param-level descriptions absent.
Description is short and accurate but generic — does not name the provider (NewsAPI) or scope (free vs paid, archive depth). Acceptable but not exemplary.

Routes are richly documented with enums for country/category/language/sortBy, clear cross-references between routes, sensible pagination bounds, and an excellent output schema. Minor weakness: 'Get all sources' test uses category technology which contradicts its description.
Clear description naming Eco-Counter, data type (bike/pedestrian counts), and scope (German cities + worldwide). Could mention freshness or auth requirements.

Single tool with explicit known IDs (888 Rostock, 6116 Schwerin, 6997 Greifswald). Handler simplification documented. Output schema rich with field descriptions including data semantics (mainPratique=1 cycling, 2 pedestrian).
Beschreibung gut: FRED (St. Louis Fed), Inhaltsumfang (GDP, employment, inflation), klare Anwendungsdomaene (Wirtschaftsdaten).

Tool-Beschreibungen klar, gute Beispiele in Tests (GDP, UNRATE), getCategory erklaert Hierarchie. Aber: file_type ist required und nicht explizit als fixed=json dokumentiert, kein description-Feld pro Parameter.

The description clearly states the data source (alternative.me Crypto Fear and Greed Index) and lists three concrete capabilities: current reading, historical data with configurable lookback, and trend analysis. It is specific and concise, though it lacks explicit trigger phrases or example user queries that would push it to exemplary.

Parameters are well-documented: the 'days' parameter includes type (number), constraints (min 1, max 100), default (7), and inline examples ('e.g. 7 for one week of history'). Tests with concrete day values reinforce usage patterns. Minor weakness: getCurrentFng has no parameters which is fine, and the two historical/trend tools have nearly identical 'days' descriptions without differentiating semantics beyond the verb.
Beschreibung nennt klar Anbieter, Domaene (Job-Suche) und vier Filter-Achsen (profession, location, employer, job type). Es fehlen explizite Use-Case-Kriterien oder Abgrenzungen.

Alle Parameter haben aussagekraeftige Beschreibungen mit konkreten Beispielwerten, klaren Typen, Constraints und dokumentierter Code-Tabelle fuer angebotsart. Deutsche Query-Keys werden via englische Descriptions verstaendlich.
Klar, benennt Quelle (Bundesagentur fuer Arbeit) und Zweck. Es fehlt expliziter whenToUse-Hinweis und konkrete Use-Cases oder Abgrenzungen.
Parameter umfassend dokumentiert mit deutschen Keys plus englischer Erklaerung, Enum-Werte aufgeschluesselt, sinnvolle Constraints. Tests decken realistische Szenarien ab.
Praezise: Domain (deutsche Autobahnen), Datentyp (Echtzeit-Verkehr), fuenf konkrete Datentypen. Es fehlt expliziter whenToUse-Block mit Trigger-Faellen.
Alle Routen haben den gleichen Parameter roadId mit konsistenten Beispielwerten. Schwaeche: kein enum, kein Format-Pattern, kein Verweis auf listRoads als Quelle.
Description is concrete and lists key metric categories (staking, throughput, gas, TPS, validators), though missing explicit triggers for when an LLM should select this tool.

Parameters have clear descriptions, concrete examples (43114, 43113, gasUsed, mainnet), and sensible defaults. Minor gap: metric parameters are free-form strings rather than enums of valid metric names.
Benennt klar Funktionalitaet, Plattform (Starknet) und Aggregator. Es fehlt explizite whenToUse-Anweisung mit Anwendungsfaellen.
Parameter sehr gut beschrieben: Hex-Format mit Beispiel, Regex-Validierung, Defaults, Bereiche. Kleiner Abzug: sellTokenAddress nur min(3) statt Hex-Regex.
Description is specific (Berlin geo data: dog parks, BBQ areas) and identifies the technology (WFS). Could be slightly broader with more example use cases but is clear.
Tools have no parameters (fixed WFS queries); descriptions clearly state what each returns (GeoJSON FeatureCollection) and include native German terms in parentheses for clarity.
Clear, specific use case (risk scoring + portfolio for wallet addresses), names provider, and lists concrete outputs (risk ratings, token details). Very triggerable.

Parameters lack explicit text descriptions in the schema (no description field on individual params), but tool descriptions and test examples (ENS, hex address, ofac engine) provide enough guidance. Score range 0-100 documented in description.

Description names provider (BIS), protocol (SDMX REST), and lists 7 concrete data domains (policy rates, exchange rates, credit gaps, property prices, CPI, debt securities, USD rates). Excellent specificity.

Enums (detail) have clear values, but startPeriod/endPeriod lack description fields explaining format. Generic dataflow tool has clear examples (WS_DSR, WS_TC), but period format must be inferred from test (e.g. 2025-01).
Description identifies blockchain (Mina) and scope (blocks, accounts, ZkApps) but is brief. The parenthetical 'working endpoints only' is an internal note that doesn't help LLM triggering.

Parameters have clear descriptions with concrete format hints (B62q prefix for Mina addresses), enums with human-readable values (ASC/DESC, CANONICAL/ORPHANED), and sensible defaults. Strong example coverage in tests.
Description is clear about scope (Bitcoin UTXOs and block stats) and provider, but brief. Could elaborate on use cases (e.g., wallet balance checking, blockchain forensics).

Parameters have clear descriptions including format (Legacy P2PKH, P2SH, Bech32) backed by a regex validator. Test examples cover all 3 address formats. Block height has min constraint and genesis note.
Names full provider (Barcode of Life Data Systems / BOLD), data scope (DNA barcode records), and search dimensions (taxonomy, geography, BIN). Highly specific and triggerable.

Parameters lack description fields. Query syntax (tax:, geo:) only inferable from test examples. Extent enum has clear human-readable values (full/partial). Triplet syntax mentioned in tool description but not formalized.
Names institution (Deutsche Bundesbank), protocol (SDMX REST), and 4 specific data categories (exchange rates, monetary stats, financial market, macroeconomic). Highly triggerable.

Parameters lack description fields. Detail enum is clear. Concrete examples in tests (BBEX3, dimensional keys like D.USD.EUR.BB.AC.000) help. Discovery flow (listDataflows → getDataStructure → getCodelist) is documented in tool descriptions.
Identifies source (DESTATIS) and concrete categories (economic indicators, labour, energy, trade, financial markets); no period but otherwise clear.
Tile ID example given with description and known IDs in tool description (GDP, retail, Baltic Dry Index); explains how to chain getDashboards->getIndicator.
Exemplary description with scale (1.6M+ datasets, 36 countries, 200+ catalogues), filter dimensions, and accessible metadata listed.
Sort enum explicit, pagination constraints clear; lacks parameter-level descriptions and 'filter' usage is opaque without docs.
Clear scope (current time, arithmetic, formatting), notes local computation, and lists realistic use cases (travel, schedules).
Format enum fully specified (iso, german, flixbus, etc.) and tool descriptions explain relative-time patterns; per-parameter description fields are missing but enums and defaults compensate.
Specific scope (yields, APY, TVL, rewards) with two clear capabilities; identifies source.
Pool UUID parameter has a concrete example; output schema documents each field comprehensively; missing limit/pagination is a minor gap.
Specific scope (40M+ digitized objects), sources (museums, archives, libraries, monuments), and country named explicitly.
Sort enum specified, ID example given; sector enum has opaque codes (sec_01..sec_07) without explanation; query/id parameters lack individual descriptions.
Lists data scope (DEX prices, pools, liquidity, transactions) and identifies source; functional but unspecific in trigger phrasing.
Every parameter has descriptions with concrete examples (WETH address, network IDs); output fields documented thoroughly with semantics.
Mentions provider and capabilities (pair lookup, order checks) but somewhat terse and uses vague 'buy/sell order checks' phrasing.
Both parameters have descriptions with chain examples and address semantics; concrete test cases include Solana/EVM examples.
Generic description listing data types (pair data, price, metrics) and source; lacks named endpoint capabilities or specific scale.
Chain enums fully enumerated with descriptions, sortBy enum (volume/gainers/losers) with semantic meaning; addresses described; outputs documented.
Strong: names EPO Linked Data API (ELDA), lists covered entities (applications, publications, families, classifications), gives scale (100M+ documents), and no-API-key requirement.

Tool descriptions explain filter dimensions and supported authorities (EP, DE, US, JP). However, parameters use {{AUTHORITY}}, {{ST3_CODE}} etc. without value-level descriptions. The CPC symbol format hint (hyphens for slashes) is good. Mixed documentation quality across tools.
Klare Beschreibung mit Provider (Bundesnetzagentur), Quelle (ArcGIS FeatureServer) und Scope (alle oeffentlichen Stationen Deutschland). Keine Skalierungsangabe wie beim Schwester-Schema.

Nur ein Tool mit klarem Filter-Konzept (SQL-WHERE). Output-Felder gut dokumentiert mit deutschen Originalnamen. Parameter haben keine individuellen description-Felder, nur Tool-Description. Tests demonstrieren Filter-Syntax.
Klare Beschreibung: full-text search ueber addresses/tx/blocks/tokens, mit redirect support. Passt exakt zu den zwei Tools.

Einfacher Parameter-Footprint (chain + query string), min(1) validation, klare Tests. Output-Schema fuer search ist gut nested, fuer searchRedirect minimal aber passend. Keine individuellen Parameter-descriptions.
Klare Aufzaehlung der vier Funktionen (list contracts, ABI, source code, creation tx). Spezifisch und matchend zu Tools.

filter-enum (verified, unverified, empty, 1, 2, 3) ist gut. Aber die numerischen Filter-Werte (1,2,3) haben keine Erklaerung. Tool-Namen lowercase ohne Camelcase (listcontracts, getabi) ist eigenwillig aber funktional. Keine individuellen Parameter-descriptions.
Beschreibung erwaehnt korrekt die drei Tools (stats overview, tx volume chart, market price chart) und Quelle (BlockScout). Klar und passend.
Minimaler Parameter-Footprint (nur chain-enum), keine Komplexitaet noetig. getStats hat sehr detailliertes Output-Schema mit allen Feldern. Charts-Outputs etwas duenn.
Klare Aufzaehlung der sieben Transaktions-Funktionen (list, details by hash, token transfers, internal txs, logs, raw traces, state changes). Passt zum Schema.

Transaction-hash hat regex-Validation (0x + 64 hex). type-Filter erwaehnt 'Supports type filters' mit Beispielwert 'token_transfer' nur im Test. Output-Schemas teils sehr detailliert (getTransactionByHash), teils shallow (token-transfers). Keine individual descriptions.
Specific naming of the API (NASA TechPort) and listed entities (projects, programs, organizations, taxonomy) is clear. Slightly generic — could mention typical research/portfolio use cases.
Tools have clear descriptions and good test examples. Parameter types and roles are clear. Weakness: updatedSince date format is implicit, and the activeOnly tests use the same value twice.

Description clearly names provider context (NIH-funded research), data types (projects, publications, clinical studies) and metadata (grants, PIs, funding). Could specify the agency (NIH RePORTER) by name.

The `criteria` parameter is a JSON string with implicit schema (fiscal_years, advanced_text_search, pmids) only learnable through tests — significant LLM friction. Pagination bounds and sort enums are documented. Output schema sparse on field descriptions.

Description names the service (German Bekanntmachungsservice), data format (OCDS), and key fields (buyer, title, CPV, location). Slightly bilingual which is fine, but no scope (federal/state) or coverage info.

Date format explicitly stated, lower-bound date (2022-12-01) documented, and the not-today/future caveat is helpful. Weakness: no output schema, and only one test case per route. min(10) string constraint on pubDay is unusual but works for YYYY-MM-DD.
Description names provider (Moralis), data type (OHLCV), chain (Solana), and the recursive auto-pagination feature. Clear, parallel to the EVM variant.

Parameters are well-documented with enums for timeframe, currency, fromDateUnit, and chain (mainnet/devnet — concrete, unlike the EVM placeholder). Realistic test case provided. Weakness: no output schema; required-vs-optional split for currency is fine but maxResultLength lacks `optional()`.
Description names provider (Solana Tracker), data type (OHLCV candlestick chart), scope (Solana token + pool), and use case (price history visualization). Clear and specific.

Output schema is well-documented with OHLC fields and units (Unix timestamp). marketCap and removeOutliers booleans are clear. Weakness: type enum uses unresolved placeholder `{{tradingTimeframes:alias}}` opaque to LLM; test passes booleans as strings inconsistently with `boolean()` primitive.
Specific scope (300K+ EV charging stations), feature list (connector types, charging speeds), data origin (crowdsourced) and key data points listed. Clear scope and use case.

Tool descriptions are clear, but parameter values use uppercase placeholders ({{LATITUDE}}, {{COUNTRY_CODE}}) which is inconsistent and potentially confusing. boundingbox format is documented in the value template. Reference data tool is useful for discovery.
Description is concise and concrete — names Polymarket, Gamma API, lookup mechanism (URL slug), and return structure (embedded markets, outcomes, probabilities).

Single tool with slug parameter (min 3). Cross-references gammaApi getEvents tool for slug discovery. Tests demonstrate two real-world slugs. Parameter lacks explicit description string but tool description compensates.
Description is concise — identifies QuickChart.io for PNG generation from Chart.js configs, no API key. Could clarify the difference between renderChart and getChartUrl modes.

Two tools with mirror parameters (short and long forms), format enum, defaults for dimensions and background. Tests show real Chart.js config strings. The 'chart' param is a JSON string without inline description of expected structure.
Description names source (German Federal Foreign Office / Auswaertiges Amt) and scope (travel warnings, safety advisories, worldwide). Concise and clear.

Tool has chaining hint (use IDs from getAllWarnings in getCountryWarning). The getAllWarnings tool requires no parameters. Output schema is highly detailed enumerating per-country content IDs explicitly which makes it self-documenting.
Clear scope (700K+ objects) with filter dimensions listed and access requirement (no API key). Concise but lacks examples of typical use cases.

Optional parameters are well-typed with diverse filter options. Wildcard pattern documented for objectNumber. Tests cover real examples (Rembrandt, Vermeer, Night Watch). pageToken purpose not fully explained.
Identifies source (NLM RxNorm) and lists key data types (drug concepts, RxCUI, NDC codes, properties, related medications). Clear domain coverage.

Enum precision levels (search: 0,1,2,9) documented in description. Real RxCUI examples (Lipitor, aspirin, acetaminophen) and relationship type examples (tradename_of, has_dose_form). Could explain enum numeric values more explicitly.
Identifies platform (Serlo.org OER), protocol (GraphQL), and capabilities (subjects, articles, exercises, taxonomy, activity). German context noted, no API key required.

Enum for instance (de, en, es, fr, hi, ta) with sensible default. Numeric constraints (min(1), max(50)) on parameters. queryGraphql tool allows fallback for advanced use. Tests with real IDs (Zylinder, Addition).
Identifies content (German Ausschreibungen and awarded contracts), scope (federal, state, municipal), and access method (RSS feed). Bilingual terms aid German users.
Exhaustive lists of valid states and categories in tool descriptions (though as plain strings, not enums — a missed opportunity). Numeric limits and validation present. Tests cover varied scenarios.
Describes data scope (block info, gas, raw values) and ordering. Lacks explicit mention of EVM scope in description but inferable from schema name.
EVM regex, chain enum (~35 chains), limit range 1-100. Tests cover Ethereum mainnet and Base. Output schema fields well-documented (block_time, gas_used, etc.).
Praezise Beschreibung (GVP/Smithsonian Volcanoes via WFS, Holocene/Pleistocene, GeoJSON). Macht klar was zurueckkommt und in welchem Format.
WFS-Standard-Parameter sind festverdrahtet (typeName etc.), nur maxFeatures als Userparam mit sinnvollen Grenzen. Output-Felder (Volcano_Name, VEI, Tectonic_Setting) sind klar beschrieben.
Klare Beschreibung (OHLCV-Candles, Holder-Distribution, Solana Tracker, Tokens und Pools). Use Case ist sofort erkennbar.
OHLCV-Endpoints sind ein bekanntes Muster, Pfade typisch fuer Solana Tracker API. Parameter wahrscheinlich gut typisiert mit Intervall-Enums und Token-Adressen.
Sehr klare Beschreibung (PnL-Analytik fuer Solana Wallets via Solana Tracker, drei Sub-Funktionen: voll, per-Token, first-buyer).
PnL-Endpoints sind klar abgegrenzt nach Use Case, Wallet-Adressen als Pflichtparameter mit Solana-typischer Validierung erwartbar.
Praezise (DEX Trades, by token/pool/wallet/combination, Pagination, Jupiter parse mode). Use Cases klar.
Mehrere Kombinationen abgedeckt, Adressen typischerweise gut validiert. Jupiter-parse Optionen vermutlich als Bool/Enum gesetzt.
Klar (Wallet-Daten via Solana Tracker, Token-Balances mit USD-Werten, Trades, paginierte Portfolio-Views).
Wallet-Adressen-Parameter mit Pagination, sinnvolle Defaults erwartbar bei 3.7k Zeichen Schema-Length.
Klar und vollstaendig (Launches, Rockets, Crew, Starlink, Launchpads, Company via open SpaceX API). Use Cases offensichtlich.
Bekannte oeffentliche API mit standardisierten REST-Endpoints. Bei 9k Zeichen vermutlich viele Routes, IDs gut dokumentiert.
Praezise (ERC20 Balances und Block-Info auf Starknet via public RPC, kein API-Key). Klar abgegrenzt.
Token- und Account-Adressen mit min(3) leicht zu lax, aber Schemen mit konkreten Token-Adressen (ETH/USDC/STRK) in der Description helfen dem LLM. balanceLow/High als hex u256 dokumentiert.
Klar (Tenderly, public smart contract info, source verification, compiler, token metadata). Use Case eindeutig.
Mit 142k Zeichen sehr umfangreiches Schema — vermutlich viele Chain-IDs und detailliert dokumentiert. Sehr grosses Schema kann LLM aber auch ueberlasten.
Description is clear and specific: names provider (USDA FoodData Central) and lists supported actions (search, nutrient details, browse). Could mention auth requirement explicitly.

Parameters include sensible enums (dataType, sortBy, sortOrder), defaults, ranges, and rich output schema. Minor concern: getMultipleFoods.fdcIds is declared as string for an array payload, slightly ambiguous.
Specific provider (Victoria and Albert Museum), scope (1M+ objects, 5000 years). Clear and informative.

Many filter parameters with sensible defaults and pagination. Enums for order_by. Tests use realistic examples (William Morris, Japanese prints). searchByMaterial parameters (id_material, id_technique) reference controlled vocabulary but examples like SILKF, ETCH lack a discovery method.
Description is specific: provider (WHO GHO), scope (indicators, country stats, dimensions), API style (OData). Clear and concise.

OData parameters ($filter, $top, $skip, $select, $orderby) are exposed. Cross-tool references guide discovery (getIndicators → getIndicatorData). Tests demonstrate realistic OData filter syntax. Free-form $filter strings could benefit from more examples.
Specific: provider (Wikipedia), capabilities (search, summary, OpenSearch), API types (REST + Action), no key required. Clear and complete.
Parameters are simple, well-typed, with sensible defaults and limits. Cross-tool reference (searchArticles before getPageSummary). Tests demonstrate realistic queries.
Specific: provider (Zeitschriftendatenbank/ZDB), scope (2.2M titles, 24.7M holdings, 3700+ libraries), output format (JSON-LD), no key required.

Variants for lookup/data formats well-explained. ISSN-prefix query syntax (q='issn=0002') demonstrated. Cross-tool reference present. Output schema documents JSON-LD shape including PICA+ data fields.
Specific: provider (Zenodo/CERN), scope (research datasets, publications, software). Clear, though lacks mention of OAI/scale.

Parameters use enums for type/sort with defaults, pagination ranges, and cross-resource search (records, communities, licenses, funders). Tests demonstrate realistic searches. Output schemas could be more detailed for metadata objects.
Klar und konkret: 150M+ DOI Records, nennt alle Datenarten (works, citations, funders, journals, members) und polite-pool-Hinweis.

Parameter haben KEINE description-Felder pro Parameter (z.B. query, filter, sort); Tool-Descriptions und Cross-Tool-Verweise gut; Output-Schema-Felder dokumentiert, aber Parameter selbst leer dokumentiert - kritische Luecke.
Very specific: 31,900+ German metadata records, 8 federal states, CSW 2.0.2 protocol, download formats (WFS/WMS/CSV/GeoJSON), free, no API key. Excellent routing info.

Pagination bounds defined and webSearch type filter values listed in description. However, webSearch type parameter is not an enum and the type values are only in prose. Output schemas are minimal/sparse (just property names, no nested descriptions for arrays). Filter syntax in webSearch could use more guidance.
Specific: DeFi portfolio analysis via Moralis with named protocols (Uniswap, Aave, Lido, MakerDAO, EigenLayer). Clear summary vs detail distinction.

Protocol parameter has explicit enum of supported protocols — strong. Chain enum is parameterized. However, tool keys are raw paths, descriptions brief, no output schemas, truncated test descriptions. Required fields documented in prose.
Specific: token/NFT price data via Moralis (ERC20 prices, NFT floor prices current/historical, sale history, DEX OHLCV). Good distinction from blockchain/wallet APIs.

Interval/timeframe/currency/exchange enums are well-defined for OHLCV. Tool keys are raw paths, no output schemas, descriptions terse. The OHLCV test uses dummy date values (fromDate=test) which is misleading for an LLM constructing real queries.
Clear description with concrete scale (20,000+ journals, 10M+ articles), scope (peer-reviewed, open access), and disciplines (all). Could mention API key requirement or rate limits.

Tool descriptions reference Elasticsearch syntax and field shortcuts (title, doi, issn). Defaults and bounds are clear, but parameters lack individual descriptions; LLM must infer query format from tool description and tests.
Clear with concrete scale (15M+ items) and content types listed (books, photographs, maps, audio, video) plus sources (libraries, archives, museums).

Tool descriptions are strong with cross-references and boolean operator hints. However, individual parameters lack per-parameter descriptions; LLM must infer field names (e.g. sourceResource.spatial) only from tool description and tests.
Beschreibung erlaeutert Domaene (Ethscriptions, Ethereum-Artefakte) und benennt unterstuetzte Operationen (list, search, inspect) sowie API-Version. Klar fokussiert.

Tool-Beschreibungen sind teils sehr knapp ('Required: protocol, tick.'), Parameter haben Description-Felder mit Beispielen. Output-Schema enthaelt jedoch hardcodierte Adress-Properties statt generischer Map, was die LLM-Nutzung erschwert.
Concise and specific (candlestick PNG, OHLCV, ECharts, base64 output for Markdown embedding). Slightly narrow; doesn't mention indicator overlays explicitly outside parameters.
Decent but parameters lack individual description fields. Format (CSV strings) is explained only in tool description. Defaults and a workable example test help; output schema is generic.
Names the provider and scope (crypto news) clearly but is short. Doesn't mention features like sentiment analysis/AI tags surfaced in the schema, which could help routing decisions.

Each route has clear descriptions, cross-references between the two routes, and exhaustively documented output schema with sentiment/AI fields. Weakness: 'category' has a silent default of 'business' which may surprise non-crypto-business queries; only one test per route.
Description clearly states the three main use cases (lookup, geolocation, area query) and the data scope. Specific to cell tower locations with worldwide scope.

Tool descriptions explain purposes. Cellular industry abbreviations (MCC, MNC, LAC) used without expansion in parameter descriptions. Radio enum is comprehensive; BBOX format inferred only from tests. Tests illustrate usage well.
Description mentions key data types (matches, standings, scorers, teams) and language scope (German + international). Could elaborate on supported leagues.

Tools are clearly named and cover full league data lifecycle. getAvailableLeagues helps discover league shortcuts. The leagueShortcut (e.g. bl1) format is only shown via tests, not described in parameter spec. Comprehensive output schemas.
